meta.title: The Fast Track Dilemma
meta.subtitle: Pre-investment decisions in a flagship corridor project
intro: You are part of the central government team preparing a flagship transport corridor.
The project will be built regardless. Your choices only determine how costly, conflictive, and fiscally painful it becomes.
Every option is defensible. That is the problem.
revealBody: This simulation was based on a real megaproject implemented in Latin America in the 2000s.

It was delivered, but costs rose far beyond early estimates, additional funding required high-level political and legislative intervention, and environmental/social issues were managed largely reactively.
The learning point is not ‘bad people’. It is how pressure, timing, and incentives make risky shortcuts feel responsible.
meta.disclaimer: Fictionalized for training. No real names are used during gameplay.

events.title: Momentum Is Already Public
context: After a regional summit, senior officials publicly committed to deliver the corridor within the current term.
Formally, the project is not yet approved. Politically, it already is.

options.text: Integrate the project into the national investment strategy and re-prioritize the pipeline transparently.
feedback: Technically sound: alignment improves coherence and opportunity-cost discipline.
Politically risky: it slows the headline and exposes trade-offs.
Irony: you have chosen truth in a world that rewards speed.

options.text: Treat it as a strategic exception and run a parallel fast-track while ‘alignment’ catches up later.
feedback: Common in practice: preserves momentum while keeping paperwork moving.
Creates a governance gap: exceptions become the new rule.
Irony: you have discovered the administrative equivalent of ‘we’ll fix it in post’.

options.text: Advance immediately; planning alignment can be done once the project is ‘irreversible’.
feedback: Fastest path to procurement; also fastest path to lock-in.
You trade decision-quality for political certainty.
Irony: you have reduced uncertainty by refusing to look at it.

events.title: Objectives: Narrow or Mythic?
context: Regional leaders insist the corridor must deliver integration, development, and national presence—not just transport efficiency.
A narrower objective might weaken the coalition supporting the project.

options.text: Define a clear transport objective and separate broader regional development programs (with their own logic and budgets).
feedback: Improves appraisal quality: you can test benefits against costs credibly.
May reduce political enthusiasm because it limits the narrative.
Irony: you chose measurable goals over inspirational ones.

options.text: Include broader development effects as core project benefits (timber/agriculture/tourism ‘uplift’).
feedback: Makes the project easier to justify with limited demand certainty.
Increases the risk of benefit overstatement and later disappointment.
Irony: your spreadsheet now contains hope with three decimals.

options.text: Keep objectives broad and intentionally flexible to preserve political support across stakeholders.
feedback: Coalition-building move: everyone can see their preferred project in it.
Accountability becomes difficult because success is undefined.
Irony: the project now has objectives like a national anthem—beautiful, and non-falsifiable.

events.title: Demand: Reality vs Narrative
context: Early political messaging assumes major international freight will use the corridor.
A technical team warns that alternative routes may remain more competitive.

options.text: Commission independent logistics and traffic studies; accept the timeline hit.
feedback: Reduces the risk of ‘build it and they will come’ appraisal errors.
May produce inconvenient results and political friction.
Irony: you asked reality for feedback. It is not always polite.

options.text: Use proxy indicators and regional integration assumptions; document uncertainty but proceed.
feedback: Looks balanced on paper and keeps momentum.
Uncertainty stays unresolved—then migrates into contract disputes and fiscal exposure.
Irony: you have carefully documented the reasons you might be wrong.

options.text: Adopt optimistic demand assumptions to avoid weakening the strategic case.
feedback: Approval becomes easier; financing becomes more ‘plausible’.
You increase the probability of later economic underperformance.
Irony: your forecast is now a political speech wearing a lab coat.

events.title: Environmental & Social: Timing the Spotlight
context: The corridor crosses sensitive ecosystems and complex social areas.
Some advisors warn that a comprehensive assessment could mobilize opposition before procurement.

options.text: Conduct a full ESIA upfront, including indirect impacts and mitigation programs.
feedback: Best practice: identifies risks early and enables credible mitigation.
Creates political exposure and potential delay.
Irony: you chose prevention, society’s least fashionable investment.

options.text: Phase the ESIA by section; focus on direct impacts first to keep procurement moving.
feedback: Common compromise: reduces immediate friction.
Indirect impacts often arrive anyway—just later and louder.
Irony: you have scheduled environmental reality for a later meeting.

options.text: Proceed with basic safeguards now; refine mitigation once construction starts.
feedback: Fastest path to breaking ground.
Highest likelihood of reactive mitigation, reputational damage, and costly adjustments.
Irony: you have successfully postponed the inevitable—at interest.

events.title: Engineering Maturity: ‘Good Enough’
context: Full engineering takes time. Approval now preserves momentum and signals seriousness.
Technical staff warn that feasibility-level costing is too thin for a megaproject in difficult terrain.

options.text: Complete full feasibility and preliminary engineering before procurement.
feedback: Improves cost accuracy and reduces scope ambiguity.
Risks losing the political window.
Irony: you chose to be right later rather than fast now.

options.text: Approve based on feasibility-level cost estimates; rely on contract management to handle uncertainties.
feedback: Keeps the process moving while ‘managing’ unknowns.
Increases likelihood of claims, re-measurement, and cost escalation.
Irony: you have promoted uncertainty to a line item.

options.text: Approve conceptually and let the contractor refine the design under time pressure.
feedback: Maximizes speed and lock-in.
You will buy information later—at monopoly prices.
Irony: you have outsourced your uncertainty to your future self.

events.title: Gatekeeping: The Standard System
context: The national public investment system requires sequential approvals and minimum documentation.
Project sponsors argue the system is too slow for ‘transformational’ projects.

options.text: Follow the standard appraisal and approval sequence without exceptions.
feedback: Strengthens institutional credibility and decision discipline.
Slows procurement and exposes trade-offs publicly.
Irony: you have chosen rules, society’s least charismatic technology.

options.text: Request fast-track treatment with partial documentation and explicit risk notes.
feedback: A middle path: keeps the system involved, but weakens its function.
Partial documentation often becomes the final documentation.
Irony: you have invented ‘controlled improvisation’.

options.text: Seek a formal exemption to bypass the system and accelerate contracting.
feedback: Fastest route; also the cleanest path to institutional lock-in.
Removes the very step designed to challenge optimism bias.
Irony: you have solved governance by deleting it.

events.title: Delivery Model: PPP as a Time Machine
context: A PPP is proposed to accelerate procurement and smooth the fiscal profile.
There is limited experience with large co-financed concessions in your context.

options.text: Choose the delivery model only after engineering maturity clarifies risk and scope.
feedback: Improves value-for-money analysis and risk allocation.
Slower; politically harder to sell.
Irony: you chose sequencing over spectacle.

options.text: Adopt a PPP early to accelerate procurement; let the concessionaire develop engineering under the contract.
feedback: Fast-track approach; common in politically driven megaprojects.
Without mature studies, risk transfer becomes either fake or expensive.
Irony: you have traded time for future renegotiations.

options.text: Choose the model that minimizes upfront budget impact, even if it increases long-term commitments.
feedback: Short-term fiscal optics improve.
Long-term fiscal exposure rises—often off-screen during approval.
Irony: you did not reduce cost; you changed when you admit it exists.

events.title: Risk Allocation Under Thin Data
context: Geological uncertainty is high; early data is incomplete.
Market sounding suggests bidders will price unknown risks aggressively—or refuse to bid.

options.text: Delay procurement to reduce uncertainty and allocate risks transparently.
feedback: Best for cost control and disputes prevention.
Politically expensive: ‘why are you slowing the flagship?’
Irony: you have decided to pay for information before paying for claims.

options.text: Retain most construction/geological risks with the public sector to keep bids competitive.
feedback: Enables procurement under uncertainty.
Transfers the downside to the budget and later parliamentary stress tests.
Irony: you have achieved competitive bids by guaranteeing you will pay for surprises.

options.text: Use flexible clauses for ‘additional works’ and remeasurement; manage through supervision later.
feedback: Feels pragmatic under uncertainty.
Creates strong incentives to expand scope and renegotiate prices.
Irony: you have written an invitation letter to future cost overruns.

events.title: Financial Engineering: Bankability First
context: A payment mechanism is proposed to mobilize domestic capital markets and ensure financing.
But payments are being locked in while costs are still uncertain.

options.text: Align financing strictly to verified cost estimates; avoid locking payments under uncertainty.
feedback: Reduces fiscal and refinancing risk.
May delay bankability and reduce political comfort.
Irony: you chose boring finance, which is the only kind that works in crises.

options.text: Structure payments to ensure bankability; accept that adjustments may be needed later.
feedback: Solves the financing problem today.
Turns information gaps into renegotiation events tomorrow.
Irony: you have converted engineering uncertainty into a fiscal schedule.

options.text: Lock in long-term payment commitments to preserve momentum and reduce investor uncertainty.
feedback: Maximizes certainty for financiers.
Minimizes flexibility for the State when costs blow past early estimates.
Irony: you have purchased certainty by selling your future options.

events.title: The Point of No Return
context: Political capital is invested. Institutions have moved. Procurement is underway.
Stopping would require more justification than continuing.

options.text: Pause and reassess scope, costs, and safeguards before final commitment.
feedback: Technically responsible: reduces lock-in and allows course correction.
Politically costly: looks like retreat.
Irony: you are about to do the hardest thing in government—change your mind.

options.text: Proceed but quietly narrow scope and add controls (without changing the headline).
feedback: A realistic compromise: protects optics while improving some fundamentals.
May not be enough if early uncertainties are large.
Irony: you have chosen ‘reform by stealth’.

options.text: Double down. The flagship must move; problems can be solved during implementation.
feedback: Maximum momentum; minimum learning.
Highest likelihood of cost escalation, reactive mitigation, and fiscal clean-up.
Irony: congratulations—you have achieved inevitability.

outcomes.title: Slow but Sustainable
outcomes.summary: You protected decision quality. The project is less glamorous on launch day, but far less painful in implementation.

outcomes.title: Bankable but Fiscally Exposed
outcomes.summary: You solved bankability under uncertainty by shifting downside to the budget. Implementation becomes a fiscal endurance test.

outcomes.title: Delivered at Any Cost
outcomes.summary: You optimized speed and procurement. The corridor gets built, and the clean-up becomes the real project.

outcomes.title: Reactive Safeguards, Escalating Conflict
outcomes.summary: Environmental and social issues arrive late, loudly, and expensively. Mitigation is reactive, not strategic.

outcomes.title: The Exception Becomes the System
outcomes.summary: Fast-tracking worked—by weakening gatekeeping. The project proceeds, but institutions lose their ability to say ‘wait’.
bullets: Future projects inherit the same shortcuts

outcomes.title: Politically Successful, Economically Fragile
outcomes.summary: You protected the political story. Later, demand and benefit shortfalls make the economic case look thinner than advertised.
bullets: Opportunity costs become visible ex post
